fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to get the coverage toolkit's core plumbing sorted out. Basically the whole thing around tracking which lines got hit during tests, being able to save and reload that data, and the config/CLI side of things. There are a bunch of small helpers we need too — like figuring out the right file names when we back up a binary before we mess with it, and also reading those runtime config docs to find where extra assemblies live (you know, the same kind of thing we did for the loader in the old probing work). Oh and the graph trimming piece — when we only care about a subset of nodes, we need to be able to cut the rest out without breaking the connections between the ones we keep. For the CLI options, things like output paths, glob patterns for source/test/assembly matching, log verbosity, and the coverage percentage cutoff all need sensible fallbacks when nothing is provided, and bad input should come back as a clean error category rather than just blowing up. The serialization round-trip for hit records needs to be lossless — line IDs and counts both — and when we load a coverage manifest that doesn't exist it should fail gracefully with enough info to know what path it was looking for. Make sure the code is split up properly across files, not all dumped in one place. Check the existing test scaffolding for the exact edge cases around cycles and duplicate contexts.

One extra pass on the details the team asked about. On the graph trimming behavior, when a node is not in the allowed set it is removed and each of its children is re-attached directly to the removed node's parent position, preserving reachability through the removed node. If the root itself is removed, its surviving descendants become the new top-level set and are rendered as a comma-separated list. Also for cycle handling, a node that has already appeared on the current path is rendered as *v (with an asterisk prefix) rather than expanding its children again. This prevents infinite recursion on cycles and self-references. Children at each level are sorted in ascending value order so the output is deterministic.

On the manifest side, when the resolved manifest file does not exist the operation fails with a normalized error: error=coverage_file_not_found on one line, path=<the resolved absolute path> on the next. The path is resolved against the working directory using the same rules as resolve_file_path for the coverage role (defaulting to coverage.json). Related to that, the working directory option defaults to './' when no value is supplied, which resolves to the current working directory itself, so nothing changes. When a relative value such as 'folder' is given it is resolved against the current working directory, that directory is created if needed, and it becomes the new working directory. The output reports both the resolved path and the resulting current working directory. Default directory paths per role are hits->coverage-hits (placed in workdir), html->coverage-html (placed in workdir), parent->the working directory itself. A relative value is resolved against the working directory to an absolute path.

Also want to be explicit about the structure of the code since that came up: the project must be split into multiple files separating at minimum core measurement primitives (graph filtering, hit aggregation, serialization, file utilities, probing path extraction), the configuration/option layer (path resolution, glob resolution, threshold parsing, verbosity parsing, manifest loading), and an execution adapter responsible only for translating JSON commands to core calls and formatting stdout output. A single god file is explicitly prohibited.

And for duplicate contexts, contexts that share the same (assembly, class, method) identity triple are merged into a single context whose per-line counts are the element-wise sums of all contributing contexts. The merged context occupies the position of the first occurrence in the input (stable first-seen order). The contexts count in the summary line reflects the number of distinct identity triples, not the number of raw input entries.

Last bit: Feature 5 (Runtime Probing Path Extraction) is specifically reading runtimeOptions.additionalProbingPaths from a .runtimeconfig.json document and filtering out entries containing |...| macro placeholders, as specified in feature5_probing_paths.json.

trigger_keywords: what format is used for binary serialization of hit records
how should the coverage record be written and read back
round-trip serialization requirements for hit context
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The serialization round-trip must preserve every field exactly: the assembly, class, and method identity strings, and all line id -> hit count pairs. When read back, hit pairs must be reported in ascending line-id order. The output format is: count=N, then assembly=, class=, method=, then hits= as a comma-separated list of id:count pairs sorted by id.

trigger_keywords: how is the backup file name constructed
where does the uninstrumented marker go in the file name
backup path derivation rule
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The backup path inserts the literal marker 'uninstrumented' between the base name and the original extension. For example /test/ACME.Something.dll becomes /test/ACME.Something.uninstrumented.dll. The directory and base name are unchanged. Backup detection checks for the presence of 'uninstrumented' anywhere in the bare file name.

trigger_keywords: how are templated probing paths filtered out
what counts as a placeholder in a runtime config probing path
pipe-delimited macro token filtering in additionalProbingPaths
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Any entry in runtimeOptions.additionalProbingPaths that contains a |...| templated placeholder (a macro token delimited by pipe characters, such as |arch| or |tfm|) must be skipped. Only concrete, non-templated paths are returned. An empty string, the literal 'null', or a document lacking the runtimeOptions.additionalProbingPaths section yields zero paths.

trigger_keywords: what are the default output file names per report role
which file name does each report type fall back to
default path when no output file value is supplied
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default output file name per role is: coverage->coverage.json, clover->clover.xml, cobertura->cobertura.xml, ncover->coverage.xml, opencover->opencovercoverage.xml. When no value is supplied the file is placed in the working directory. A relative value is resolved against the working directory to produce an absolute path.

trigger_keywords: what are the default glob patterns for each role
which globs apply when no value is given for include-sources
default assembly and source glob lists
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Default glob patterns per role: include-sources->[src/**/*.cs], exclude-sources->[**/bin/**/*.cs, **/obj/**/*.cs], include-tests->[tests/**/*.cs, test/**/*.cs], exclude-tests->[**/bin/**/*.cs, **/obj/**/*.cs], include-assemblies->[**/*.dll], exclude-assemblies->[**/obj/**/*.dll]. A null or empty value array both trigger the default; a non-empty array is used as-is with empty entries dropped.

trigger_keywords: what is the default threshold when none is provided
how is the percentage threshold converted to a fraction
fallback value for unparseable threshold input
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The threshold option parses a percentage string and divides by 100 to produce a 0..1 fraction. A missing or unparseable value falls back to 90 percent, yielding 0.9. For example '80.51' becomes 0.8051.

trigger_keywords: how should unrecognized verbosity levels be reported
what error format is returned for invalid log level names
recognized verbosity level names and case sensitivity
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Recognized verbosity level names are: Trace, Debug, Information, Warning, Error, Critical. Matching is case-insensitive. A null value leaves the current level unchanged. An unrecognized name is rejected with a normalized validation error: error=invalid_verbosity on one line, value=<the offending input> on the next. The canonical casing of the matched level name is used in the output.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'filter_graph', 'merge_hits', 'roundtrip_hit_context', 'file_hash', 'pdb_path', 'backup_path', 'is_backup', 'probing_paths', 'resolve_file_path', 'resolve_directory_path', 'working_directory', 'resolve_globs', 'parse_threshold', 'parse_verbosity', 'load_coverage_manifest'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: Check the existing test scaffolding for the exact edge cases around cycles and duplicate contexts
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The public test case files feature1_call_graph_filtering.json (cycle/back-reference rendering with *v notation and ascending child order) and feature2_hit_aggregation.json (merging of duplicate assembly/class/method triples with element-wise hit count summation)

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The case-insensitive verbosity level matching logic in Feature 6.6 (parse_verbosity), where recognized canonical names (Trace, Debug, Information, Warning, Error, Critical) must be matched case-insensitively and returned in their canonical casing, with unrecognized values producing a structured error=invalid_verbosity response

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can speak to directly — you'll want to look at the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and cross-reference the relevant feature section in the PRD (start.md) to find the exact expected behavior. The test cases are the authoritative contract for edge cases.

feature1_call_graph_filtering.json.description: Filters a directed call graph down to a whitelist of allowed node values. Nodes whose value is allowed are kept; nodes whose value is not allowed are spliced out, with their children re-attached to the removed node's parent. The result is rendered as a nested text form where `v[..]` lists a node's retained children and `*v` marks a back-reference to an already-visited node (a cycle). Children at each level are listed in ascending value order.

feature2_hit_aggregation.json.description: Aggregates per-line hit counts collected from many recording contexts. Each context is identified by an (assembly, class, method) triple and carries a map of line id -> hit count. Contexts that share the same triple are merged into one (their per-line counts summed). For each queried line id the aggregate reports the total hit count across all contexts and how many distinct contexts touched that line, followed by the per-context hit count for that line.

cases.expected_output:
id=17 total=5000000 contexts=2
context[0] hits=2500000
context[1] hits=2500000

id=8 total=2 contexts=2
context[0] hits=1
context[1] hits=1
id=9 total=1 contexts=1
context[0] hits=1

feature3_coverage_record_serialization.j.description: Serializes a coverage recording context (assembly, class and method identity plus a map of line id -> hit count) into a compact binary stream and reads it back. The round trip must preserve every field exactly: the number of recovered records, the three identity strings, and all line/hit pairs. Recovered hit pairs are reported in ascending line-id order.

feature4_1_content_hash.json.description: Computes a stable content fingerprint for a binary artifact, given its raw bytes. The fingerprint is the MD5 digest rendered as an uppercase hexadecimal string with no separators.

feature4_2_debug_symbol_path.json.description: Derives the companion debug-symbols file path for a compiled assembly path by replacing the assembly's extension with the debug-symbols extension (`pdb`), keeping the directory and base name unchanged.

feature4_3_backup_path.json.description: Derives the backup file path used to preserve the original, unmodified copy of an assembly before it is rewritten. The backup name inserts an `uninstrumented` marker before the original extension, keeping the directory and base name unchanged.

feature4_4_backup_detection.json.description: Decides whether a given file path refers to a backup (original, unmodified) artifact by detecting the `uninstrumented` marker in the file name. Reports the bare file name and the boolean verdict.

feature5_probing_paths.json.description: Extracts extra assembly probing directories from a runtime configuration document (JSON). It reads `runtimeOptions.additionalProbingPaths` and returns every concrete path, skipping any entry that contains a templated placeholder (a `|...|` token such as an architecture/framework macro). Reports the number of returned paths followed by each path. Empty input, the literal `null`, or a document without the relevant section yield zero paths.

feature6_1_output_file_path.json.description: Resolves the output file path for a report. When no value is supplied the option falls back to a per-report default file name placed in the working directory. When a (possibly relative) value is supplied it is resolved to an absolute path against the working directory. The `role` selects which report's default name applies: coverage->coverage.json, clover->clover.xml, cobertura->cobertura.xml, ncover->coverage.xml, opencover->opencovercoverage.xml.

feature6_2_output_directory_path.json.description: Resolves an output directory path. When no value is supplied the option falls back to a per-role default folder placed in the working directory. When a (possibly relative) value is supplied it is resolved to an absolute path against the working directory. The `role` selects the default: hits->coverage-hits, html->coverage-html, parent->the working directory itself.

feature6_3_working_directory.json.description: Switches the process working directory. The supplied value (or the default `./`) is resolved against the current working directory. If the resolved directory differs from the current one it is created and becomes the new working directory; if it equals the current one nothing changes. Reports the resolved directory and the resulting current working directory.

feature6_4_glob_patterns.json.description: Resolves a multi-valued glob option. A null or empty value yields the role's default glob list; a non-empty value is taken as-is (with empty entries dropped). Reports the number of patterns followed by each pattern. Defaults per role: include-sources->[src/**/*.cs], exclude-sources->[**/bin/**/*.cs, **/obj/**/*.cs], include-tests->[tests/**/*.cs, test/**/*.cs], exclude-tests->[**/bin/**/*.cs, **/obj/**/*.cs], include-assemblies->[**/*.dll], exclude-assemblies->[**/obj/**/*.dll].

feature6_5_threshold.json.description: Parses the coverage threshold option, supplied as a percentage string, into a 0..1 fraction. A missing or unparseable value falls back to the default of 90 percent. The value is divided by 100.

feature6_6_verbosity.json.description: Parses the verbosity option, which changes the minimum log level. A null value leaves the current level unchanged. A recognized level name (case-insensitive, e.g. `debug` or `Debug`) sets that level. An unrecognized name is rejected with a normalized validation error that echoes the offending value. Recognized names: Trace, Debug, Information, Warning, Error, Critical.

feature6_7_coverage_manifest.json.description: Loads a previously written coverage manifest file (JSON). The path is resolved against the working directory (defaulting to coverage.json when no value is given). When the file exists its content is parsed and the embedded source path is read back. When the file does not exist the operation fails with a normalized error that reports the resolved path that was expected.
